body_text:
5
RESIDUE THEORY
After having thought on this subject, and brought together the div results mentioned above, I had the hope of establishing on a direct rigorous analysis the passage from the real to the imaginary; and research has lead me to this Memoire.
-Augustin Louis Cau
[Writing about his Memoire of 1814, which contained the residue orem and several computations of real integrals by complex metho

In the previous four chapters, we introduced complex numbers complex functions and studied properties of the three essential too calculus: the derivative, the integral, and series of complex functi In this and the next chapter, we use these results to derive some ex ing applications of complex analysis. The applications of this cha are based on one formula, known as Cauchy's residue theorem. have already used this result many times before when computing i grals. Here we will highlight the main ideas behind it and devise techniques for computing important integrals that arise from Fou series and integrals, Laplace transforms, and other applications. example, integrals like $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x, \int_{0}^{\infty} \cos x e^{-x^{2}} d x$ are very diffi to compute using real variable techniques. With the residue theo and some additional estimates with complex functions, the compt tions of these integrals are reduced to simple tasks.

In Section 5.7 (an optional section), we will use residues to exp our knowledge of analytic functions. We will use integrals to co the number of zeros of analytic functions and to give a formula for inverse of an analytic functions. Theoretical results, such as the o mapping property, will be derived and will be used to obtain a fr and different perspective on concrete results such as the maxim modulus principle.

The residue theorem was discovered around 1814 (stated explici in 1831) by Cauchy as he attempted to generalize and put under umbrella the computations of certain special integrals, some of th involving complex substitutions, that were done by Euler, Lapl Legendre, and other mathematicians.
